sections.title: Inversions with negative adverbial words and phrases

content.subtitle: Form
items: **No sooner had I booked** the flight than they told me it had been cancelled.

rows.label: Never
items: **Never have I travelled** so far in all my life.

rows.label: Rarely
items: **Rarely have I travelled** so far in all my life.

rows.label: Seldom
items: **Seldom have I travelled** so far in all my life.

rows.label: No sooner (… than)
items: **No sooner had Matt arrived** at the airport **than** he missed his flight.

rows.label: Hardly (… when)
items: **Hardly had Matt arrived** at the airport **when** he missed his flight.

rows.label: Not only (… but also/too)
items: **Not only were you** late for the departure, **but** you had **also** forgotten your passport.

rows.label: Under no circumstances
items: **Under no circumstances are passengers allowed** to leave the gate area without permission.

rows.label: At no time/point
items: **At no time/point was I told** what the tour would involve.

rows.label: Little
items: **Little did I realise** that I would end up visiting fifteen countries just two years later.

rows.label: Not until
items: **Not until the next morning did I hear** that my lost luggage had been found.

rows.label: Only
items: **Only at the end of the trek did I think** I had a chance of reaching the summit.

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: *Little* is used with verbs of thought (*realise*, *know*, *suspect*, etc) and means 'I did not realise/etc at all'.
✓ **Little did I know** how challenging the journey was going to be.
(= I did not know at all how challenging the journey was going to be.)
With *not until* and *only*, you have to be careful about which verb to invert.
✓ **Not until I had packed** my bags **was I allowed** to leave the hostel.
✗ Not until had I packed my bags was I allowed to leave the hostel.
✓ **Only when I had packed** my bags **was I allowed** to leave the hostel.
✗ Only when had I packed my bags I was allowed to leave the hostel.

rows.label: In short answers and other similar structures using *so*, *neither* and *nor*
items: 'I'm a tour guide.' 'Really? **So am I**!'
My sister doesn't enjoy long-haul flights, and **neither do I / nor do I**.

rows.label: After *as*, *so* and *such*
items: The guide was nervous about the group's arrival, **as were the rest of the porters**.
**So late was it** that there was no one left at the campsite.
**Such a hot day was it** that no one wanted to continue hiking.

rows.label: In conditional sentences
items: **Were our equipment** better maintained, we might have a safer expedition. (= If our equipment were better maintained ...)
**Had I known** about the detour, I would have taken a different route. (= If I had known ...)

content.subtitle: Be careful
content.description: Remember that '*So am/do/have I*' is used to agree with a positive statement and '*Neither/Nor am/do/have I*' is used to agree with a negative statement.
examples: ✓ 'I really don't enjoy long layovers.' '**Neither do I / Nor do I**.'

rows.label: We use 's with singular nouns, including names, and with irregular plurals which do not end in -s.
items: That's the tour guide**'s** map, over there.
We are still waiting for Sarah**'s** decision about which route to take.
I think that women**'s** rights should be respected everywhere.

rows.label: We just add an apostrophe to regular plural nouns ending in -s.
items: The travellers**'** bags were checked at customs.

rows.label: We use 's or s' in some time expressions.
items: After an hour**'s** walk, I finally reached the scenic viewpoint.
I'll be at the hostel in about ten minutes**'** time.

rows.label: We usually use 's or s' with people (or groups composed of people) and animals. For other things, we normally use *of the/my/etc*.
items: Is this John**'s** backpack?
We held a meeting to discuss the agency**'s** itinerary.
The vet had a close look at the cat**'s** paw.
The mechanic had a close look at the frame **of my** bicycle. (not ...at my bicycle's frame)

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: With singular names ending in -s, some people use 's and some people just add an apostrophe. Both are correct, although using 's is more common.
✓ The itinerary is on Charles**'s** tablet in the tent.
✓ The itinerary is on Charles**'** tablet in the tent.
With plural names ending in -s, we just add an apostrophe, as with other plurals.
✓ The Smiths**'** travel agency eventually closed down.
With phrases, the possessive 's and s' must go at the end of the whole phrase.
✓ Tom, Dick and Harry**'s** backpack is around here somewhere.
✗ Tom's, Dick's and Harry's backpack is around here somewhere.
✓ The President of France**'s** visit to England will be good for tourism.
✗ The President's of France visit to England will be good for tourism.

rows.label: Possessive determiners (*my*, *your*, *his*, *her*, *its*, *our*, *their*) come before a noun and show possession
items: I'm really excited about **my** upcoming adventure.
Are you looking forward to meeting **your** new travel companions?

rows.label: Possessive pronouns (*mine*, *yours*, *his*, *hers*, *ours*, *yours*, *theirs*) are used instead of a noun
items: My trip was amazing. How about **yours**? (= ...your trip)
Our journey is going well, but I hear Simon and Julie are having problems with **theirs**. (= ...their journey)

rows.label: Possessive pronouns can also be used after *of* to show possession
items: She's a fellow traveller of **mine**. (= ...one of my fellow travellers)
Are they tour guests of **yours**?

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: A common mistake is using a possessive pronoun instead of a possessive determiner or using a determiner instead of a pronoun.
✓ I don't really get on so well with **my** travel partner.
✗ I don't really get on so well with **mine** travel partner.
✓ I know your route is dangerous, but did Frank and Jenny tell you about **theirs**?
✗ I know your route is dangerous, but did Frank and Jenny tell you about **their**?
Remember that there are no apostrophes in possessive pronouns.
✗ I know your route is dangerous, but did Frank and Jenny tell you about **their's**?
Don't get confused between *its* (possessive determiner, without an apostrophe) and *it's* (contraction for *it is* or *it has*, with an apostrophe).
✓ Our travel agency is hoping to increase **its** share of the market.
✗ Our travel agency is hoping to increase **it's** share of the market.
